questions.prompt: What does 'Why did this render?' in React DevTools tell you?

choices.text: The cause of each render: which props changed, whether hooks fired, whether the parent re-rendered (no prop change), or context updated. The killer feature for performance diagnosis — points you at the right fix. 'Parent rendered' with stable-looking props means a reference-identity issue (new object every render). 'Hooks changed' means a state/reducer update. Identifying the cause leads directly to the right intervention.

questions.explanation: Tells you the cause of each render — props, hooks, parent, context.

questions.prompt: Page slow with 1,000 visible items. Best fix?

choices.text: Virtualize the list — render only ~20 items currently visible (plus a small overscan), reuse DOM as the user scrolls. Libraries: TanStack Virtual, react-window, react-virtuoso. Memory and CPU stay flat regardless of list length. React.memo alone doesn't solve the 'too many items rendering' problem; pair memo with virtualization once items render only when visible.

questions.explanation: Virtualization for long lists. React.memo alone doesn't address volume.

questions.prompt: When does React.memo actually save renders?

choices.text: When the parent re-renders frequently, the child is genuinely expensive, AND the child's props are STABLE references across parent renders. Without stable refs, React.memo's shallow equality fails (new object/function each render), child re-renders despite memo. Pair memo with useMemo / useCallback at the parent to stabilize props. If any of the three conditions don't hold, memo adds overhead without benefit.

questions.explanation: Frequent parent + expensive child + stable props. Otherwise pure overhead.

questions.prompt: Component has 10+ useMemo/useCallback 'for safety.' Keep them?

choices.text: Probably not. Each has overhead: storing values, comparing deps every render, memory for the cache. For cheap computations or callbacks not passed to memoized children, overhead exceeds savings — net negative. Profile to see which actually save measurable time; remove the rest. React 19's React Compiler auto-memoizes correctly. For 18-: memoize when profiling shows a real bottleneck, not preemptively.

questions.explanation: Many memos add cost without saving. Profile and keep only the ones that pay off.

questions.prompt: Initial bundle 800KB; 300KB is charting library used only on /dashboard. Fix?

choices.text: Lazy-load components that use the chart library: `const Chart = lazy(() => import('./Chart'))` with Suspense fallback. The library ships in the lazy chunk, loaded when /dashboard renders. Other routes drop 300KB from initial bundle. Combined with route-level splitting (automatic in Next.js App Router; manual via React Router lazy), the bundle savings compound. Re-run bundle analyzer to verify.

questions.explanation: Lazy-load components that use heavy libraries; the library ships in the lazy chunk.
